section.title: Woe to the City of Blood
section.sectionRationale: The book's final oracle, a sustained woe over Nineveh. Nahum indicts the city of blood, exposes her as a seductive harlot, points to fallen Thebes as her mirror, and mocks her merchants and officials as locusts that vanish. It ends not with hope for Nineveh but with the applause of all who had suffered her endless cruelty.
section.thesis: Nineveh, the bloody city of lies and seduction, will fall as surely as Thebes did; there is no healing for her wound, and all who hear of it will clap, for none escaped her constant cruelty.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the woe over the city of blood; God exposing the harlot; the warning from fallen Thebes; the locust hordes that flee; and the final dirge over an incurable wound.

units.label: Woe to the city of blood
units.note: the whip, the wheel, the heaps of slain

verses.text: {{hoy|Woe}} to the city of {{dam|blood}}, full of lies, full of plunder, never without prey.
The crack of the whip, the rumble of the wheel, galloping horse and bounding chariot!
Charging horseman, flashing sword, shining spear; heaps of slain, mounds of corpses, dead bodies without end, they stumble over their dead,

units.label: God exposes the harlot
units.note: I am against you, declares the LORD

verses.text: because of the many harlotries of the {{zanah|harlot}}, the seductive mistress of sorcery, who betrays nations by her prostitution and clans by her witchcraft.
“Behold, I am against you,” declares the LORD of Hosts. “I will lift your skirts over your face. I will show your nakedness to the nations and your shame to the kingdoms.
I will pelt you with filth and treat you with contempt; I will make a spectacle of you.
Then all who see you will recoil from you and say, ‘Nineveh is devastated; who will grieve for her?’ Where can I find {{nacham|comforters}} for you?”

units.label: Learn from Thebes
units.note: are you better than the city on the Nile

verses.text: Are you better than Thebes, stationed by the Nile with water around her, whose rampart was the sea, whose wall was the water?
Cush and Egypt were her boundless strength; Put and Libya were her allies.
Yet she became an exile; she went into captivity. Her infants were dashed to pieces at the head of every street. They cast lots for her dignitaries, and all her nobles were bound in chains.
You too will become drunk; you will go into hiding and seek refuge from the enemy.

units.label: Fortresses fall, locusts flee
units.note: ripe figs into the mouth, merchants like locusts

verses.text: All your fortresses are fig trees with the first ripe figs; when shaken, they fall into the mouth of the eater!
Look at your troops, they are like your women! The gates of your land are wide open to your enemies; fire consumes their bars.
Draw your water for the siege; strengthen your fortresses. Work the clay and tread the mortar; repair the brick kiln!
There the fire will devour you; the sword will cut you down and consume you like a young locust. Make yourself many like the young locust; make yourself many like the {{arbeh|swarming locust}}!
You have multiplied your merchants more than the stars of the sky. The young locust strips the land and flies away.
Your guards are like the swarming locust, and your scribes like clouds of locusts that settle on the walls on a cold day. When the sun rises, they fly away, and no one knows where.

units.label: An incurable wound
units.note: all who hear applaud your downfall

verses.text: O king of Assyria, your shepherds slumber; your officers sleep. Your people are scattered on the mountains with no one to gather them.
There is no healing for your injury; your wound is severe. All who hear the news of you applaud your downfall, for who has not experienced your constant cruelty?

hoy.gloss: Woe, alas, an exclamation of grief and doom often used to open a prophetic funeral lament. To pronounce hoy over a city is to announce its death in advance. Nahum opens his final oracle with this cry, framing all that follows as a dirge over Nineveh.

dam.gloss: Blood, and by extension bloodshed or guilt for murder. The Hebrew phrase is literally city of bloods, a plural pointing to countless killings. Nineveh is named for its defining sin: the shedding of innocent blood on a massive scale.

zanah.gloss: To play the harlot, to prostitute oneself. Nahum casts Nineveh as a seductive prostitute who lured and betrayed the nations, selling them enslavement dressed up as alliance. The image exposes Assyria's diplomacy and idolatry as spiritual and moral corruption.

nacham.gloss: To comfort, console, or have compassion. It is the very root of the prophet's own name, Nahum, meaning comfort. In a piercing irony, God asks where any comforters can be found for Nineveh: the city that showed no mercy will receive none, while the same word of comfort belongs to God's people.

arbeh.gloss: The swarming locust, a devouring horde that strips a land bare and then vanishes. Nahum likens Nineveh's merchants, guards, and officials to locusts: numerous, greedy, and gone in a moment when the sun rises. What seemed an unstoppable multitude simply flies away.

basic.context: This closing chapter is one long woe over Nineveh, the capital of Assyria. Nahum calls it the city of blood, full of lies and plunder, and pictures the carnage of its own conquests now turned back on itself. He reminds Nineveh of Thebes, a mighty city on the Nile that also fell, and mocks its officials as locusts that swarm and then vanish.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a prophetic funeral song for a real city that fell in 612 BC. It is not celebrating cruelty; it is declaring that the God of justice will finally repay a regime built on blood. The chilling climax is that no one will mourn Nineveh, because everyone had felt its endless cruelty.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter opens with hoy, woe, the cry of a funeral. Nineveh is the city of dam, bloods, marked by murder. She is a harlot, zanah, who seduced and betrayed the nations. And in a sharp irony God asks where comforters, from the root nacham, the very meaning of Nahum's name, can be found for her.
basic.typology: Nineveh the bloody harlot foreshadows Babylon the great in Revelation, the seductive city drunk on the blood of the saints, whose fall the whole world witnesses. Fallen Thebes and fallen Nineveh together warn every proud power that no wall or wealth can save it from God's justice.
basic.christ: The blood-guilt of Nineveh could never be healed; its wound was incurable. But the gospel is that there is healing for the guilty who turn to Christ, whose own blood was shed to cleanse the very sins that condemn. Where Nineveh found no comforter, Christ is the true comfort for all who repent, even the worst of sinners.
basic.god: We learn that God takes bloodshed and cruelty with deadly seriousness, that He exposes and shames what is proud and corrupt, and that His justice is inescapable. The mercy He longs to give, comfort, is refused only by those who refuse to repent.
basic.crossReferences: Revelation 18 shows the fall of Babylon the great, the final city of blood. Genesis 9 declares God's reckoning for shed blood. And Jesus offers rest and comfort to all who come to Him, even those with a past like Nineveh's.
basic.oneStory: The dirge over Nineveh belongs to the story of God bringing down every violent, seductive power that oppresses His people, from Nineveh to Babylon the great. Against that dark backdrop, the offer of true comfort and cleansing in Christ shines all the brighter.

context.literary: Chapter 3 is a sustained taunt-song and funeral lament, opening with the classic prophetic hoy, woe. Nahum moves through rapid battle sounds, a courtroom-style exposure of the harlot, a historical warning, and mocking similes of figs and locusts, before closing on a note of grim finality. The imagery is deliberately shaming: skirts lifted, nakedness displayed, filth thrown.
context.historical: The reference to Thebes, the Hebrew No-amon, is a dated marker: the Egyptian city fell to Ashurbanipal of Assyria in 663 BC, an event still fresh in memory. Assyria itself was famed and feared for its documented atrocities, flaying captives, impaling, and mass deportation, which is why Nahum can call it a city of blood and expect readers to agree. Nineveh fell in 612 BC, as he foretold.
context.canonical: The chapter's imagery of the harlot city drunk and stripped, whose fall the nations witness, feeds directly into Revelation's portrait of Babylon the great, Revelation 17-18. Nahum thus stands in a line of oracles against proud world-cities, from Babel to Babylon, that culminates in the final judgment of the God-defying city.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To pronounce God's final, just sentence on a regime built on bloodshed and deceit, to vindicate the countless victims of Assyria's cruelty, and to warn every reader that no strength saves the proud from the God who repays.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The oracle describes God's judgment and the world's relief at Nineveh's fall; it does not prescribe gloating or human vengeance. The abiding truths are the seriousness of bloodshed, the certainty of God's justice, and the availability of comfort only through repentance.

debates.question: Should we be troubled that all who hear applaud Nineveh's downfall, 3:19?

views.label: Righteous relief of the oppressed
views.case: The applause is the sigh of countless victims finally vindicated; it celebrates the end of a blood-soaked tyranny, not personal spite, and voices the biblical longing that justice be done, as in the cry of the martyrs, Revelation 6:10.

views.label: A sober warning against vindictiveness
views.case: Others caution that the verse reports the world's reaction rather than commending gloating, and that Scripture elsewhere warns against rejoicing when an enemy falls, Proverbs 24:17; the believer grieves sin even while trusting God's justice.

debates.bearing: Both guard against opposite errors. God's judgment is genuinely good news for the oppressed and a real vindication of justice, yet it is never an invitation to personal cruelty; the cross shows God's own grief over sin even as He judges it.

study.originalLanguages: The oracle is a funeral song, opening with hoy, woe, the cry raised over the dead. Nineveh is the city of dam, literally the city of bloods, the plural marking murder heaped on murder. She is exposed as a zanah, a harlot, whose treaties and idolatry were seduction and betrayal. Her officials and merchants are arbeh, swarming locusts, a numberless horde that strips the land and then flies away at sunrise. And at the center stands the sharpest irony of all: God asks where any nacham, comforter, can be found for her, using the very root of the prophet's name, Nahum, comfort. The city that gave no mercy will find none.

typology.fulfillment: Babylon the great, the harlot drunk on the blood of the saints, whose fall the world witnesses, Revelation 17-18.

typology.type: The incurable wound with no healing, 3:19
typology.fulfillment: By contrast, the healing offered in Christ, by whose wounds sinners are healed, Isaiah 53:5, so that the unpardonable becomes pardonable through repentance.
Christ the true comforter and God who sends the Comforter to His people, John 14:16, a mercy Nineveh forfeited by refusing to repent.

study.christ: Nineveh's wound was incurable; its blood-guilt could not be washed away, and no comforter could be found. That is the terror of unrepented sin, and it makes the gospel astonishing. For the blood of Jesus cleanses the very guilt that condemned Nineveh, and by His wounds even the cruelest sinner may be healed, Isaiah 53:5. Where Nineveh found no comforter, Christ Himself is our comfort and sends the Comforter to all who turn to Him. The difference between Nineveh and the forgiven is not the size of the sin but the presence or absence of repentance and faith.
study.god: The LORD holds nations accountable for bloodshed and treats cruelty and deceit as capital crimes against His throne. He exposes and shames the proud, stripping away the glamour of empire to reveal its corruption. His justice is thorough and inescapable, an incurable wound for the impenitent. Yet the very name of the prophet, comfort, testifies that the God who judges the oppressor is the same God who consoles His people and stands ready to comfort any who repent.

crossReferences.note: God requires a reckoning for the shedding of human blood, the principle behind the woe on the city of blood.
A caution not to gloat when your enemy falls, balancing the relief at Nineveh's downfall.
By His wounds we are healed; the healing Nineveh could not have is offered in Christ.
The fall of Babylon the great, the seductive city of blood, fulfilling the pattern Nineveh embodies.

study.oneStory: The funeral song over Nineveh completes the story Nahum has been telling and joins the greater biblical account of God toppling every violent, seductive power that oppresses His people, all the way to Babylon the great. Set against that darkness, the meaning of Nahum's own name shines: comfort. The God who leaves the impenitent city without a comforter is the God who is Himself the comfort of all who turn to Christ.

quiz.q: Why is Nineveh called the city of blood in verse 1?

options.text: The Hebrew reads city of bloods, marking Assyria's massive, defining guilt of shed innocent blood.
options.note: The plural points to murder heaped on murder, the empire's characteristic sin.

options.text: Because much blood was shed in her many wars and conquests.
options.note: On the right track: it refers to bloodshed, specifically Nineveh's guilt for shed innocent blood.

options.text: Because it was built beside a red river.
options.note: The reference is to violence and killing, not geography.

options.text: It is an empty insult with no basis.
options.note: Assyria's documented atrocities fully ground the charge.

quiz.q: Why does Nahum point Nineveh to the fall of Thebes in verses 8-10?

options.text: As a warning: a mighty, well-defended city already fell, so Nineveh is not safe either.
options.note: Thebes, with the Nile as her wall, still fell; so will Nineveh.

options.text: To praise Thebes as greater than Nineveh.
options.note: The point is not praise but warning by parallel.

options.text: To recall a great city Assyria itself conquered, now a warning of Nineveh's own turn.
options.note: True that Assyria took Thebes; the point is the warning that Nineveh will fall in the same way.

options.text: It is an unrelated historical aside.
options.note: It is a deliberate warning that no fortress guarantees survival.

quiz.q: What is the striking irony in God's question, where can I find comforters for you, in verse 7?

options.text: It uses the root of Nahum's name, comfort; the city that gave no mercy will receive none.
options.note: The prophet of comfort announces that no comfort remains for the impenitent city.

options.text: It means God is desperately searching for someone to help Nineveh.
options.note: It is a rhetorical question declaring that no comforter will be found.

options.text: It is a rhetorical question whose answer is that none are left to comfort her.
options.note: Right that none will comfort her, but it also plays on Nahum's name, comfort.

options.text: It has nothing to do with the prophet's name.
options.note: Nahum means comfort, and the wordplay is deliberate.

quiz.q: What do the locust images in verses 15-17 say about Nineveh's officials and merchants?

options.text: They are a greedy, numberless horde that strips the land and then vanishes at sunrise.
options.note: The locust pictures multitude and plunder followed by sudden disappearance.

options.text: They are hard-working farmers feeding the city.
options.note: The locust devours and flees; it is a picture of plunder, not provision.

options.text: They are a vast multitude, but one that plunders and then abandons the city.
options.note: Right that they are a great multitude; the sting is that they strip the land and flee.

options.text: They guarantee the city's permanence.
options.note: The locusts flee and no one knows where; nothing is guaranteed.

quiz.q: How does Nineveh's incurable wound point us to Christ?

options.text: Its blood-guilt could not be healed, but Christ's wounds heal even the guiltiest who repent, Isaiah 53:5.
options.note: The difference is repentance and faith, not the size of the sin.

options.text: It warns that unrepented sin stays incurable, though Christ heals all who turn to Him.
options.note: Close: impenitence leaves the wound incurable, but the sin's size is no barrier to Christ.

options.text: It shows God never really heals anyone.
options.note: The gospel offers real healing to all who turn to Christ.

options.text: It has no connection to the gospel.
options.note: The contrast with Christ's healing wounds is the very point.

questions: Nahum names Nineveh's defining sin plainly: blood, lies, and plunder. What sin, if named honestly, would define the pattern of your own life, and what would it mean to bring it to God?
Thebes seemed unconquerable, yet it fell. What are you leaning on as though it could never fail, and how does Nineveh's warning reorder your trust?
God asks where comforters can be found for Nineveh, and the answer is nowhere, because she would not repent. How does the door of repentance still standing open for you move your heart today?
The world applauded when the cruel empire fell. How do you hold together honest longing for justice with the warning not to gloat over any person's downfall?
Nineveh's wound was incurable, yet by Christ's wounds even the worst sinner can be healed. Is there a guilt you have thought beyond forgiveness, that the blood of Jesus is able to cleanse?

translationNotes.note: City of blood renders the Hebrew city of bloods, the plural indicating repeated, massive bloodshed rather than a single act.
Thebes is the Hebrew No-amon, the great Egyptian city on the Nile that fell to Assyria in 663 BC, cited as a fresh and undeniable example of a mighty city brought down.
Guards and scribes render Hebrew terms that may also mean princes and marshals; the point is that Nineveh's officials, however titled, scatter like locusts at sunrise.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Nahum 3:1-19. Poetic line fragments joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
